result:
The first time I saw the ocean I was twenty-three, which is too late for something that size. It should be part of the municipal curriculum: every seven-year-old marched to the edge of the continent and told “this is what’s waiting when you learn to stay alive.” Instead I arrived as an adult, luggage in tow, shoes filling with cold wet sand like somebody had tipped the hourglass over and forgotten to warn me. I remember thinking: so this is the thing that’s been breathing on the other side of the hills my whole life. It didn’t look real; it looked like a screensaver someone had flung across the world while God was in the bathroom.  

I kept meeting people who talked about the sea as if it were a pet they’d trained: “She’s calm today,” they said, the way you’d say “good dog.” But no one warned me that it growls in its sleep, that it rehearses nightmares on the moon’s rehearsal stage, that it will pitch forward, all foam and white eyes, and try to collect the ankles of anyone who forgets to respect the script. I stood ankle-deep, felt the drag sucking the sand from under my arches, and understood for the first time what it means to be small in a measurable way. Not metaphor, not philosophy—just cubic meters of water outweighing my bones by orders of magnitude too boring to write down.  

Back home I own three houseplants and a kettle that shrieks at the smallest provocation. I can walk from one end of the apartment to the other in nine steps if I angle my feet like a ballet dancer who’s given up on joy. The rooms smell of cardamom and the persistent ghost of an ex who brewed coffee dark enough to write warnings in. I keep the windows open so the air remembers to move. At night the traffic lights cycle through their private traffic-light dreams, spilling red-yellow-green across the ceiling like a secret code I’m too tired to break.  

I’ve been trying to teach myself to whistle for fifteen years. My mouth makes a sort of asthmatic breeze, the sound of a balloon apologizing. Sometimes I dream I can do it perfectly: three crisp notes that part the clouds and somewhere a dog sits up, alerted. I wake disappointed, tongue dry, lips cracked like old paint. But I keep practicing because failure is a muscle and muscles fascinate me—the way they tear to grow, the way they remember insults, the way they can cramp in the dark and convince you you’re dying until you stand up and blood revisits the negotiation table.  

When I was eight I believed the moon followed me personally, a private investigator hired by God to keep files. I waved at it from the backseat, certain it recognized the gesture. Years later a drunk astronomer at a party told me the moon is actually spiraling away from us at the rate fingernails grow. I laughed too loudly because I pictured the moon in a station wagon, arm out the window, accelerating toward the dark convinced it’s escaping, while we down here clip our nails and sweep the clippings into little crescent graves.  

I keep a list of things that surprise me:  

1. How heavy a dry towel becomes when you’re exhausted.  
2. The way grocery-store tomatoes still hold the July sun if you close your eyes.  
3. That people can survive airplane crashes and still be afraid of spiders.  
4. That my father’s voice mails compress eternity into forty-five seconds.  
5. That coins in a jar will eventually weld together out of loneliness.  

The list grows, never alphabetized, because I want to remember that order is a choice and most days I choose not.  

Sometimes I ride the subway end to end, no book, no phone, letting the city rehearse its humanity pageant. A woman clips her fingernails into a potato-chip bag; the sound is a tiny guillotine. Two teenagers speak entirely in song lyrics, fluent in billboard romance. A man in a suit cradles a cactus like it’s newborn royalty. I sit in the bright aquarium of the carriage and feel the ordinary miracle rubbing against my shins. We emerge above ground and the whole car inhales light as if we’d forgotten what lungs were for. Then back underground, a collective sigh, the way you close the oven door on something rising.  

I read once that the average person spends six months of life waiting for red lights to turn green. I try to use mine: I practice gratitude, or count prime numbers, or rehearse apologies I’ll never deliver. Red is the color of the universe holding up a hand, saying pause, there will be time to barrel forward later, but right now just breathe with me. I breathe, and the guy in the pickup breathes, and the nervous dog in the crosswalk breathes, and for three seconds we are a congregation of mammals temporarily free of momentum.  

At night I write letters to my future children I may never have. I tell them:  

—Your mother learned to swim in borrowed water.  
—Your first word was “more,” but pronounced like a question.  
—The year you turned four we danced in the kitchen until the lightbulb joined in.  
—I kept every drawing you ever made, even the one where my head is a square.  

I store the letters in an old shoebox that once held running shoes I never ran in. The paper is starting to yellow, like it’s competing with my memory to see which can oxidize faster.  

Once a month I visit the planetarium, sit under the perforated dome, and wait for the guide to dim the lights so I can cry in public without scandal. Stars appear like gossip overhead. The narrator says, “We are made of elements forged in stellar furnaces,” and I feel suddenly legitimate, like a document finally notarized by the cosmos. The tears are part homesickness, part relief: imagine being allowed to belong nowhere and everywhere simultaneously, a citizen enrolled by carbon.  

Afterward I walk home past closed florists and open bars, past bus shelters advertising vacations I can’t pronounce, past the laundromat where a man folds hospital scrubs the way another man might fold origami cranes—precise, reverent, as if each crease keeps someone alive. I unlock my apartment, greet the plants by the names I give them (Frida, Bashō, Aretha), kettle on, shoes off, moon hopefully watching from the alley.  

And then I sit at the table and write, because the ocean is still doing whatever the ocean does—breathing in, breathing out—unconcerned with anyone’s first sightlines. Because the city outside is humming like a hive that’s forgotten the word for disaster. Because somewhere a dog is lifting its head to a whistle I haven’t learned to blow. Because the kettle is warming to the idea of screaming, and I am warming to the idea of staying alive long enough to answer.
